data.title: Bessent: Instead of buying toys for kids, contribute to 'Trump accounts'
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Wednesday that relatives of children should donate to their new “Trump accounts” as opposed to buying them a toy. 

“Relatives, rather than giving a toy for a birthday, for a holiday, they can contribute to these accounts,” Bessent said on “Fox and Friends.” The Treasury secretary also called contributions to the accounts a “new kind of philanthropy.”

Bessent and President Trump touted the tax-free accounts, also called Section 530A accounts, during a Wednesday summit. Established under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act that Trump signed into law last July, the accounts are available for children who do not turn 18 before the end of the calendar year in which their parents open an account, according to the IRS.

Additionally, Treasury is offering to contribute $1,000 to accounts for children born from Jan. 1, 2025, through Dec. 31, 2028. Starting July 5, individuals — including family members, friends and other adults — can contribute up to $5,000 per year to an account, a cap that will increase with inflation starting in 2028. Employers of the child or their parent can also contribute up to $2,500 annually, which counts against the $5,000 limit.

Charitable organizations and state governments may also make contributions, which do not count against the annual limit. 

The funds in the account have to be invested in certain mutual funds or exchange-traded funds that track the S&P 500 or “another index of primarily American equities,” the IRS says.

Parents can open an account for their child via elections on IRS Form 4547. Their children can access the funds in their account on Jan. 1 of the year they turn 18.

During the summit, Bessent said the accounts “will turn simple investments into life-changing money” that children can use to pay for college, buy their first home or save for retirement.

“Trump accounts are a generational downpayment on the American dream,” he added.

The White House Council of Economic Advisers (CEA) projected in August that, under average stock market conditions, accounts for babies born this year will grow to more than $5,800 by the time they turn 18, without contributions. Under the same stock market conditions but with maximum contributions, those accounts will accrue to more than $303,000 in value, CEA projects.

data.title: Walz: Trump compared Minnesota crackdown to Venezuela operation
data.description:
Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) on Wednesday said President Trump compared his federal immigration crackdown in the North Star State to the U.S. military operation in Venezuela that ousted President Nicolás Maduro.

“He told me that he doesn’t understand what’s wrong with Minnesota,” Walz told MS NOW’s Jacob Soboroff, recalling his call with Trump on Monday. “‘I don’t know what’s wrong with you people.’ I said,’ Nothing’s wrong with us, Mr. President. We’re one of the most effective states and one of the best places to live.'”

“And he told me that, ‘Well, look, Tim, we did this in New Orleans. We did it in Louisville. There’s no problems,'” Walz continued. “And I said, ‘You didn’t kill anybody in Louisville or New Orleans. And the operation here looks very different from that.’ And and then he told me it was successful in Venezuela.”

Soboroff pressed on Trump’s comparison, to which Walz said he was unsure why, with “what’s happening to my state, that I’m interested in Venezuela.”

“But he told me how well that went,” the Minnesota governor added. “Which really was strange to me was he saw an operation in Venezuela against a foreign nation in the same context he saw an operation against a U.S. state and a U.S. city.”

Walz told Soboroff that Trump’s call was about “needing something from us,” and that Trump called because “his poll numbers dropped and it looked bad on TV.” The governor added that Trump never mentioned the names of Alex Pretti or Renee Good, two U.S. citizens killed during federal immigration operations in Minneapolis this month.

The Hill reached out to the White House for comment.

Trump said he and Walz had a “very good call,” which was requested after a second U.S. citizen, Alex Pretti, was killed after an altercation with federal officers, fueling anger with the administration’s aggressive immigration crackdown.

“I told Governor Walz that I would have Tom Homan call him, and that what we are looking for are any and all Criminals that they have in their possession,” Trump wrote on Truth Social on Monday. “The Governor, very respectfully, understood that, and I will be speaking to him in the near future. He was happy that Tom Homan was going to Minnesota, and so am I!”

Homan, the White House border czar, said during a Thursday briefing that federal immigration operations in Minnesota were undergoing internal changes after Good and Pretti’s deaths. Trump dispatched Homan on Monday.

Minnesota elected officials at the state and local level have called on the Trump administration to withdraw federal immigration officers from the state following Good and Pretti’s death. The tide shifted among some Republicans after the second death, with Sen. Bill Cassidy (R-La.) saying that the credibility of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) “are at stake,” and two other senators suggesting that DHS Secretary Kristi Noem should be removed from office.

Senate Democrats are threatening to block a government-spending bill that would fund DHS, increasing the chances for a partial government shutdown. Some Senate Republicans are willing to leave out the DHS funding bill for now, but House Republicans warn that any changes to the bill would not pass in the House.

data.title: Campaign finance corruption in New York: Leftist attack on political speech continues
data.description:
This week marks 50 years since the U.S. Supreme Court’s decision in the landmark political speech case of Buckley v. Valeo.  

This case established that expenditures by political candidates, campaigns and committees constitute political speech protected by the First Amendment, and therefore could not be subject to spending limits.

Similarly, the court held that contributions to campaigns, although not given as broad of protection as expenditures, could only be capped for the limited purposes of combatting corruption or the appearance of corruption. Contribution limits to “level the playing field” for underfunded candidates, according to the Supreme Court, violated the First Amendment.

In the five decades since the Buckley decision, the Supreme Court has consistently upheld the rights of those who seek to speak about candidates, politics and public policy. Later decisions by the high court, such as Citizens United, and lower court decisions like Speechnow.org v. Federal Election Commission, continued the trend toward increased constitutional protection for political speech, regardless of the identity or funder of the speech. 

Progressives continue their hostility to these decisions, as well as the larger framework established by the Buckley decision, to this day. But although progressive politicians constantly carp about Buckley, Citizens United and money in politics generally, they haven’t been shy in using the few remaining tools at their disposal to undermine the political speech protections established by the courts.   

New York, more than any other state, has served as the left’s laboratory for end-runs around the free speech regime established by Buckley. These efforts, however, have rarely accomplished — and likely won’t accomplish —  the goals they initially set out to achieve.

For the progressive left in the Empire State, the crown jewels of these “reforms” are New York’s generous public campaign finance programs. New York City’s program was established in 1988, but New York State’s is a more recent innovation, quietly slipped into a budget bill in the early days of the COVID-19 lockdown. It had its trial run in the 2024 state legislative races, and in 2026, it will also be available for statewide candidates. 

Although the program has only had one opportunity to prove itself, it is, so far, falling short of what its backers promised.

One participant, an Assembly candidate from Queens, has been charged federally with orchestrating a fraudulent donor scheme to illegally obtain more than $150,000 in public matching funds. Another 2024 participant, a state Senate candidate from Central New York, is alleged to have used homeless people as straw donors to qualify. The Senate candidate’s campaign allegedly traded gift cards for campaign donations.

But the state program’s problems go beyond scams to illegally receive public matching funds. What the program permits is often just as odious. In the 2024 cycle, dozens of state Senate candidates and more than 100 Assembly candidates were able to claim public matching funds for donations made by their spouses and family members. While this might be legal, it’s hard to see it as anything more than a way for candidates, consultants and campaign workers to game the system for more public campaign dollars. And it certainly doesn’t fit with the stated intentions of the progressive activists who spent years advocating for New York state to adopt a public campaign finance program. 

Even the Democratic state commissioners who oversee the state public finance program have been accused of improperly tinkering with the system to provide a benefit for their party’s candidates. After the 2024 election concluded, the body that administers the program, the Public Campaign Finance Board, on a party-line vote approved a resolution retroactively allowing candidates in the program to transfer public funds without penalty to party committees. These transfers were, in some circumstances, over $100,000.

The much older New York City public finance program also has a checkered history. For instance, the campaign of 2013 New York City mayoral candidate John Liu was embroiled in a scandal to obtain public matching funds through fraudulent contributions. Liu, who is now a New York state senator from Queens, was never charged with a crime, but his campaign was fined by the New York City Campaign Finance Board. Other members of Liu’s campaign team were tried and convicted of federal wire fraud, obstruction of justice, and other crimes associated with their involvement in the scheme. 

More recently, a scandal surrounding the New York City public finance program reached all the way into the office of New York’s lieutenant governor. Brian Benjamin, under Gov. Kathy Hochul’s first lieutenant governor, was charged by federal prosecutors for his alleged involvement in a fraudulent donation scheme to benefit his campaign for New York City comptroller. Benjamin, who resigned immediately after being charged, eventually saw the charges against him dropped, but this occurred only after a key witness in the case died.  

It’s hard to see how the Empire State’s “reforms” have been a success. New Yorkers continue to have a low opinion of their state government, and persistent pessimism in the state’s direction. Corruption in government continues, and the public funding of elections has, almost certainly, opened the door for even more corruption.  

New York’s progressives spent years searching for workarounds to Buckley. They found their antidote in the state and city public campaign finance programs.

But as the 50th anniversary of Buckley approaches, and as the Supreme Court signals a willingness to expand its political speech protections in upcoming decisions, New York’s progressive left might pause to ask whether their counters to Buckley have accomplished anything beyond enriching political consultants and creating new opportunities for the corruption they claim to oppose. 

Joe Burns is a partner with the Holtzman Vogel law firm, with a focus on election cases in New York State. He previously served as deputy director of election operations at the New York State Board of Elections.

data.title: ACA enrollment drops by 1M+ after subsidies expire
data.description:
Affordable Care Act enrollment dropped by over a million after enhanced subsidies expired last year, causing monthly insurance premiums to skyrocket. 

The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) on Wednesday reported that 23 million people signed up for open enrollment, which concluded on Jan. 15. It marks a drop off from the 24.2 million people who enrolled for insurance during the same period in 2025.

Last year marked four consecutive years of record enrollment for ACA Marketplace plans. Gains were credited to enhanced premiums tax credits signed into law under former President Biden through the American Rescue Plan and extended by the Inflation Reduction Act.

Heightened prices reflect the expiration of the COVID-19 pandemic-era tax credits, which kept health insurance costs lower for struggling Americans. The issue was a major flashpoint in spending talks last year and the turmoil resulted in a 43-day record-long government shutdown.

Democratic lawmakers have pushed for a three-year extension of the subsidies, but the House-passed bill has stalled in the Senate.

Health care advocates have decried the decision to allow premiums to skyrocket, citing harsh conditions that may require citizens to choose between food and medical care.

“Having folks get off the system will derail the entire health care marketplace, especially as more people will unenroll after they receive their first bill,” Rep. Adam Smith (D-Wash.) said in a statement this week, noting thousands in his home state are now uninsured because they “cannot make ends meet.”

His concerns were echoed by Kevin Patterson, CEO of Connect for Health Colorado, who said he’s seeing similar drop offs.

“On one hand, it’s encouraging to see enrollment remain relatively steady, with only a slight dip, and to see so many people receive meaningful financial assistance…,” Patterson said in a statement. “On the other hand, it’s deeply troubling that a record number of people are canceling their plans because they simply can’t afford their monthly payments, or are being forced to choose between health care and basic necessities like housing and food.”

He added, “Without federal enhanced Premium Tax Credits, this alarming trend is, sadly, emerging across the country.”

The latest numbers reflect the data from the 30 states that use the federal ACA Marketplace. Some states that rely on their own independent exchanges have open enrollment until later this week, which could affect the enrollment numbers.

“These numbers are going to go dramatically down month after month as people decide that they can’t afford health care and stop paying their premiums,” Leslie Dach, chair of Protect Our Care, an advocacy group, said in a statement, according to CNN.

data.title: Rosen: Trump administration 'out of control,' 'running around like they are kings'
data.description:
Sen. Jacky Rosen (D-Nev.) went after the Trump administration Wednesday, saying it is “out of control” and accusing officials of “running around like they are kings.”

“They are running roughshod through our own country and through others. And Donald Trump and his cruel, tyrannical Cabinet are out of control,” Rosen said in a clip posted by C-Span to the social platform X, mentioning U.S. strikes on alleged drug boats.

“And Marco Rubio — what is he? Jobs of everything and oversight of nothing,” she added later.

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the 12:30 Report newsletter

President Trump has seen his favorability take a hit in recent months amid controversy over both domestic and foreign policy.

In the last few months, the administration has faced heat over affordability, questions about strikes on alleged drug boats in the Caribbean and Pacific, scrutiny over the release of files linked to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein and blowback over immigration raids.

In a recent poll, Trump’s backing by independent voters fell to a fresh low for both of his terms. The Economist/YouGov poll found 27 percent of independents approving of his job.

Rosen also recently called for the impeachment of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem in the wake of a second shooting death at the hands of federal immigration authorities in Minneapolis last weekend.

“Kristi Noem and her department’s latest attempt to mislead the American public regarding the brutal and unjustified killing of Alex Pretti is deeply shameful, and she must be impeached and removed from office immediately,” Rosen said in a Sunday statement.

The Hill has reached out to the White House and State Department for comment.

data.title: EPA proposes to limit states' 'Good Neighbor' obligations to reduce interstate smog pollution
data.description:
The Trump administration is moving to limit obligations on certain upwind states to cut smog pollution from their power plants that can flow to downwind states.

The Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on Wednesday proposed “phase 1” of its rollback of the Biden-era “Good Neighbor” plan.

The “Good Neighbor” regulations broadly seek to prevent pollution in one state from preventing downwind states from meeting federal air quality standards.

As part of its phase 1 effort, the Trump administration would approve state-level plans from eight states that the Biden administration sought to reject as insufficient.

“Today, we are taking an important step to undo a Biden Administration rule that treated our state partners unfairly,” EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin said in a written statement.

The Biden administration sought to impose strict limits on smog-forming nitrogen oxide pollution for 23 states’ power plants, pipelines, cement-makers, steel mills, chemical companies and more.

It said at the time that it expected its action would create significant public health benefits, including the prevention of 1,300 premature deaths in the year 2026.

The Supreme Court halted the Biden plan while challenges to it played out in lower courts. But now, the Trump administration is taking matters into its own hands and indicated last year that it viewed the Biden plan as too broad and planned to narrow it.

Under Wednesday’s action, Alabama, Arizona, Kentucky, Minnesota, Mississippi, Nevada, New Mexico and Tennessee would not need to further restrict nitrogen oxide pollution beyond what they’ve laid out in their plans to the EPA.

Environmental advocates slammed the Trump administration’s move. 

 “Once again, Donald Trump and Lee Zeldin are choosing to protect aging, dirty and expensive coal plants and other industrial polluters over strong federal clean air protections that address interstate pollution problems,” said Sierra Club senior attorney Zachary Fabish in a written statement.

data.title: House Republicans unveil election reform bill dubbed 'Make Elections Great Again Act'
data.description:
House Republicans on Thursday unveiled a sweeping elections reform bill that would mandate long-sought requirements like photo ID when voting and citizenship verification to register to vote. 

Dubbed the “Make Elections Great Again (MEGA) Act,” the bill is led by House Administration Committee Chair Bryan Steil (R-Wis.) and marks an evolution of election reform legislation that House Republicans have crafted in previous congresses, such as the American Confidence in Elections (ACE) Act.

“Americans should be confident their elections are being run with integrity – including commonsense voter ID requirements, clean voter rolls, and citizenship verification,” Steil said in a statement. “These reforms will improve voter confidence, bolster election integrity, and make it easy to vote, but hard to cheat.” 

In addition to requiring photo ID when casting a ballot and citizenship verification upon voter registration, the bill implements restrictions on how states administer and maintain their election systems — some of which became flashpoints in the 2020 election, fueling President Trump’s claims of election fraud.

The bill would require mail-in ballots to be received by the close of polls on election day to be counted, making an exception for members of the military stations overseas. Several states currently count mail-in ballots to be counted if received up to a certain point after Election Day as long as they are postmarked by election day — often leading to delays in determining election winners in states like California where the vast majority of ballots are cast by mail.

It would require states to use auditable paper ballots for a federal election. The vast majority of federal ballots are already cast on paper, with a 2024 Brennan Center analysis estimating that 98 percent of ballots would use paper ballots in that year’s presidential election.

It would also implement stronger routine voter list maintenance requirements, and ban universal vote by mail systems by requiring voters to affirmatively request a ballot to vote by mail.

“Ballot harvesting,” the practice of collecting and delivering completed ballots and delivering them to polling centers, would also be prohibited for anyone who is not an immediate family member or caregiver of an individual. It would also prohibit a person from distributing or delivering more than four mail-in ballots at a time.

The bill would prohibit federal agencies from using taxpayer funds to promote voter registration, in reference to former President Joe Biden’s 2021 executive order directing agencies to help the public register to vote, with Republicans deeming the practice “BidenBucks.”

The bill would also ban ranked choice voting in federal elections. The system, in which voters rank their top choices of candidates rather than voting for a single person, is used in some statewide elections in Maine and in Alaska and a smattering of local elections across the country.

Unveiling of the bill comes a day after Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) told reporters that another GOP elections bill that would require proof of citizenship to register to vote, the Safeguard American Voter Eligibility (SAVE) Act, will come to the Senate floor “at some point.” The bill has previously passed the House, and Thune said lawmakers are working to add a requirement ensuring only residents can cast a ballot, not just register.

The Make Elections Great Again Act is endorsed by numerous conservative election reform activists.

“I’ve spent the last decade registering voters across the country and empowering Americans to vote. Without fair elections, we aren’t a free nation,” Scott Presler, founder of Early Vote Action, said in a statement. “Thank you to Chairman Steil for his leadership on comprehensive election reform. Let’s make elections great again!”

It also got stamps of approval from Jason Snead, director Honest Elections Project Action; J. Christian Adams, President of the Public Interest Legal Foundation and Commissioner on the United States Commission of Civil Rights; and Justin Riemer, president and CEO of Restoring Integrity and Trust in Elections.

data.title: Life expectancy in United States hits all-time high: CDC
data.description:
People in the United States are living longer than ever before, according to new data from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC).

A report released Thursday by the CDC’s National Center for Health Statistics (NCHS) found that the life expectancy of a person living in the U.S. was 79 years old in 2024, an increase of half a year compared to 2023.

The report indicated that females continue to live longer than males, with female life expectancy increasing from 81.1 years in 2023 to 81.4 years in 2024. Life expectancy jumped slightly more for males, going from 75.8 years in 2023 to 76.5 years in 2024.

The difference in life expectancy between the two sexes decreased to just under five years, according to the data.

Death rates also fell by nearly 4 percent nationwide, dropping to their lowest level since before the COVID-19 pandemic. In 2024, 3.07 million deaths were registered in the United States, just over 18,000 fewer deaths than in 2023.

The most significant drop in death rates was in Hispanic males at 5.9 percent, followed by American Indian and Alaska Native females and males at 5.2 percent and 5.1 percent, respectively.

The leading causes of death were heart disease, cancer, and unintentional injuries, which together account for approximately 70 percent of all deaths in the U.S. in 2024; however, rates decreased for each of these causes, contributing to overall higher life expectancy.

Suicide became the 10th leading cause of death, following a drop in pandemic-level deaths. The number of deaths for which COVID-19 was the underlying cause decreased approximately 37 percent from 49,932 in 2023 to 31,426 in 2024.

The number of deaths attributed to COVID-19 dropped by 37 percent in 2024, with data showing the virus was the underlying cause in 31,426 deaths. The pandemic was replaced as the 10th leading cause of death by suicide.

The data found no significant change in the infant mortality rate. The number of infant deaths was 20,050 in 2024, only 95 fewer than in 2023, and the leading causes of death remained largely the same.

The report was compiled using data collected by NCHS from death certificates filed in all 50 states and the District of Columbia.

data.title: Congress must intervene to protect NATO and Greenland
data.description:
In his addled and bellicose Davos World Economic Forum speech, President Trump threatened U.S. takeover of Greenland to satisfy his self-admitted psychological “need” to own and control the North Atlantic island, which comprises 97 percent of the territory of the Kingdom of Denmark. Predictably, his cascading threats blindsided our NATO allies, further undermining their trust in the United States.

Later that same afternoon, however, when challenged by these European leaders and with his eye on financial markets over his political weaponization of associated tariffs, the mercurial Trump reversed course. He claimed victory by announcing he had a “framework of a future Arctic deal” on Greenland with our NATO partners to protect the Arctic region.

Really?

To ensure the U.S. doesn’t back into a further confrontation with our NATO allies over his insatiable desire to acquire more real estate — or something worse — Congress needs to reassert its constitutional powers as a separate but equal branch of government and intervene to oversee any enhanced Arctic security agreement or amended treaty with Denmark on Greenland.

On July 21, 1949, the U.S. Senate ratified the NATO treaty by a 82-13 vote, meeting the required two-thirds majority. It thereby became part of “the supreme law of the land” according to the Constitution, which also requires the president to faithfully execute treaties approved by the Senate. The U.S. is thus a founding member of an alliance that has produced an era of unprecedented relative peace and economic prosperity among its members for over three-quarters of a century. NATO marked the first peacetime U.S. military alliance outside the Western Hemisphere.

Denmark’s claim to Greenland dates to 1721, a period of 305 years. The U.S. didn’t exist then. It has no claim to Greenland, as Trump has teased. The United Nations General Assembly officially recognized Greenland as part of the Kingdom of Denmark in 1954.  

In 1946, President Harry Truman tried to buy Greenland from Denmark for $100 million in gold. Then, in 1951, the U.S. recognized the sovereign claim of the Kingdom of Denmark over Greenland in the Defense of Greenland Agreement. In so doing, it solidified U.S. military defense rights there in the post-war world. To this day, the United States has a military base and presence there, which can be the foundation for future additional mutual cooperation to enhance NATO security in the Arctic region to counter any perceived or real threats from Russia or China.

In 2017, the president offered an even more moronic plan for snatching Greenland. After hurricanes had devastated the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico, a self-governing U.S. territory, where 3 million American citizens live, Trump proposed that because Puerto Rico was “poor and dirty,” the United States should just simply trade it for Greenland, without regard for the lives and future of American citizens living there. Trump insisted he “needed” Greenland then as well.

President Trump does not “need” Greenland. What the U.S. does need is a continuing, peaceful, mutually cooperative relationship with Denmark and all NATO members — precisely, in part, to ensure mutual security in the Arctic. We do not need a hostile takeover by an authoritarian president without congressional involvement and in violation of the binding agreements found in the U.N. Charter and NATO treaties.

Any invasion or coercive takeover and control of most of another NATO ally’s territory, without provocation, not only violates international law but also would mimic what Russia has done in Ukraine.

Before any “framework of a future deal” proceeds, Congress must ensure that the United States doesn’t stumble into an avoidable conflict as the result of a president who is clearly spinning out of control. Both strong statements by leading congressional Republicans immediately following the president’s threats and the bipartisan introduction of the NATO Unity Protection Act in both the House and Senate are good first steps.

Denmark’s allies are right to be concerned and prepare to defend Greenland under NATO’s Article 5, just as Denmark and other NATO allies came to the defense of the United States in the wake of the tragedy on U.S. soil on Sept. 11, 2001.

To avoid any further unilateral threats that needlessly raise international tensions, Congress must intervene and fully exert its constitutional authority to oversee any future framework or amended treaty on Greenland. We need to reassure our NATO allies that the United States is a valued partner they can fully trust.

Marc Racicot is former U.S. Army Captain, JAG Corps officer, Montana Special Prosecutor, state attorney general, governor and chair of the Republican National Committee. Greg Wilson is former deputy assistant secretary at U.S. Department of the Treasury.

data.title: Nicki Minaj says she got 'Gold Trump card free of charge' after praising president
data.description:
Rapper Nicki Minaj said Wednesday she received a “gold card” from President Trump, hours after praising the president during an event touting his administration’s child investment accounts. 

Minaj posted a photo of the immigration card, emblazoned with Trump’s face on it, to social platform X with the caption “Welp.”

She later wrote on X that she is “finalizing that citizenship paperwork as we speak as per MY wonderful, gracious, charming President.” She added that she received the card, which typically costs $1 million for individuals, free of charge. The cards, created via a September executive order, provide an alternative pathway to citizenship for highly skilled foreign workers.

“I wouldn’t have done it without you. Oh CitizenNIKA you are thee moment,” the singer said. 

Minaj, born in Trinidad and Tobago, wrote on Facebook in 2018 that she came to the U.S. illegally when she was 5 years old.

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment. 

During the Treasury Department summit promoting the new “Trump accounts,” Minaj appeared on stage alongside the president, even holding his hand while businessman Kevin O’Leary spoke. Trump said Wednesday that the rapper plans to donate hundreds of thousands to the accounts of her fans, although the final amount and qualification details are unclear. 

While at the podium, Minaj called herself “probably the president’s No. 1 fan.”

“And that’s not going to change,” she continued. “And the hate or what people have to say, it does not affect me at all. It actually motivates me to support him more, and it’s going to motivate all of us to support him more.”

Trump also praised Minaj, saying, “I just think she’s great” and joking that he is going to let his nails grow long like hers.

“I didn’t know Nicki, and I’ve been hearing over the years she’s a big Trump supporter, or Trump fan. And she took a little heat on occasion because her community isn’t necessarily a Trump fan,” he said.
